channel.title: shell on ilo
channel.description: Recent content in shell on ilo

item.title: Customize Environment
item.description: The ilo shell command uses the fedora:latest image by default. In most cases fedora:latest will not be enough to compile/test/package/run your software. While you can install additional packages inside the container, those changes will be lost once you remove the container. In contrast to toolbox, ilo relies on immutable containers. ilo allows you to define your build environment either in a Dockerfile or any other OCI Image compliant way. Make sure your image can be accessed by everyone in your team and use ilo shell your.

item.title: Examples
item.description: The following examples show how ilo shell can be used. Cargo Projects Cargo caches all downloaded dependencies in your local ~/.cargo directory. In order to re-use already downloaded dependencies inside the container, specify a --volumne like this: # Gradle project that mounts local .gradle folder $ ilo shell \ --volume $HOME/.cargo:/root/.cargo:Z \ rust:latest Gradle Projects Gradle caches all downloaded dependencies in your local ~/.gradle directory. In order to re-use already downloaded dependencies inside the container, specify a --volumne like this:

item.title: Options
item.description: The ilo shell command can be configured with the following command line options. From your terminal, use ilo shell --help to get a list of all options, and their default values. --context The --context option allows to specify the context when building an image with --dockerfile. # specify custom context $ ilo shell --context ./local/folder # do not specify context $ ilo shell By default, --context is set to . (the current directory).

item.title: Runtimes
item.description: ilo shell by default searches your local system for supported runtimes. It prefers podman over docker over lxd at the moment. In order to force the usage of a specific runtime, use the --runtime flag. Docker Force ilo to use docker like this: $ ilo shell --runtime docker # alias $ ilo shell --runtime d Important: In case your image uses root as its user, and you cannot run rootless docker (introduced with 19.
